title: truncate_content rewrites line endings (CRLF→LF) and drops trailing newlines silently
description:
## Severity
Trivial
## Dimension
kiss
## Location
src/memdir/entrypoint.rs:137-148 (truncate_content)
## Violation
Line-truncation path rebuilds content via `lines().take(N).collect::<Vec<_>>().join("\n")`:

```rust
let mut truncated = if line_count > MAX_ENTRYPOINT_LINES {
    lines_triggered = true;
    let kept: Vec<&str> = raw.lines().take(MAX_ENTRYPOINT_LINES).collect();
    kept.join("\n")
} else {
    raw.to_string()
};
```

Side effects the caller does not expect:
1. CRLF line endings in the source file become bare LF in the truncated output. Windows-authored MEMORY.md files are silently rewritten.
2. A trailing newline on the original file (very common — git diff trims trailing-newline-less files) is dropped, because `lines()` does not yield a final empty item for a terminal `\n`.
3. The suffix `\n\n[truncated — …]` is appended unconditionally, so when only the byte limit fires (line_count ≤ MAX_ENTRYPOINT_LINES), the leading line endings of the original are preserved but the tail gets the same LF-only suffix — inconsistent line-ending style within one file.

None of these is a security issue but they violate the round-trip invariant and make it surprising when an exact-match comparison against a CC-canonical MEMORY.md fails on Windows checkouts.
## Mandated refactor
1. Track line offsets into the original slice and emit `&raw[..end_offset]` rather than re-joining — preserves the original line endings and any trailing newline.
2. Emit the suffix with the same line-ending style detected in the body (sniff `\r\n` vs `\n` from the first line break).
3. Test: feed `b"a\r\nb\r\nc\r\n"` × 201 and assert the output still contains `\r\n`.
## Filed from QA review dispatch
